summary: Chris Riccomini provides an update on his various projects after a summer hiatus from newsletter writing. He has been working on the second edition of Designing Data-Intensive Applications, specifically rewriting the batch chapter and updating the streaming chapter. SlateDB development continues with new features like serializable snapshot isolation and transactions, plus Python and Go bindings. The most exciting SlateDB news is ZeroFS, a POSIX-compliant network filesystem built on top of SlateDB. His venture fund Materialized View Capital is 75% deployed and will be fully deployed in 18 months.
key_points: The batch chapter of DDIA second edition required a full rewrite while streaming updates will be more minimal, reflecting the stagnant streaming ecosystem
SlateDB has added serializable snapshot isolation, transactions support, Python and Go bindings, and a deterministic simulation tester
ZeroFS is a new POSIX-compliant network filesystem built on SlateDB that will enable zero-copy filesystem forking
Materialized View Capital is 75% deployed and will be fully deployed ~18 months from inception, ahead of the typical 3-year target
The author uses coding agents constantly and plans to write more about AI, MCP, and enterprise agent adoption
money_quotes: The updates to the streaming chapter reflect this; they'll be more minimal.
ZeroFS provides network filesystem (NFS), network block device (NBD), and Plan 9 Filesystem Protocol (9P) implementations on top of SlateDB.
SlateDB's branching and forking features mean ZeroFS will be able to provide zero-copy filesystem forking—an important feature for AI and many other use cases.
I've been reluctant to post about the topic because I don't feel I'm an expert in the subject. (Then again, it's so new that very few are.)
I use coding agents constantly, though.
An 18 month deployment for a smaller fund like MVC is not unheard of.
key_insight: The streaming ecosystem has been remarkably stagnant over the past decade, while the most exciting infrastructure innovation is happening at the storage layer with projects like SlateDB enabling new possibilities like zero-copy filesystem forking.
